speaker: interviewer
text: --seven interview with Mr. Zoltan Wieder. So you were able to get some of your things back. How did your neighbors react to you coming back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, the neighbors, some of the neighbors-- I got back a little later. But when my brother got back, he got back in October, November '44 already. And the some two other friends of his, and they were very shocked, some of them. They didn't expect the Jews to come back, anybody. And probably, they were afraid. And they were-- some of them. But some of them, they reacted, oh, I'm sorry about to you, and what's happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your mother, father, and sisters? And some of them, they were asking us. But some of them, they were-- but some of them, I'm sure they were not that-- What did you do in Baia-Sprie?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time when I came back, I was thinking, you know, I wanted to continue my studies, and also to make a living. I was alone. And my brother had already something. You know, with-- with two friends, he opened a store. So I said to myself, let me do something with my-- in my father's store, which was a smaller place. The place was still there. And another man, who was a friend of my father, he-- because during the Hungarians, my father has very big problems with the store. He Didn't have enough money to run it. So he rented it out to that guy, to the other guy, during the-- between '40 and '44. So this guy came back also.

speaker: interviewee
text: He escaped. He was in labor camp also, like my brother. And he said to me, you know what? Let's open that store, partners. So we opened that store-- he, me, and myself. And-- and my brother helped. He had a little money, you know. We didn't need too much money, because he still had some merchandise there from before the war. It was there. So we opened. And that store was on his name, not on my name. And I was lucky it wasn't on my name, because under the communists, I would be persecuted if you had something before. So-- and then we had it for about from '46 to '48, because in '48 they nationalized everything, the communists that took over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went out from that store. And I let-- my partner was still there for a year or two. And then he had to close the store. And then I went to work for the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: As what?

speaker: interviewee
text: As-- I was working in the forest industry as a technician, and a clerk, in a government job, because everything was government, and-- down in Baia-Sprie. I was also studying. Try to-- to go back-- to go to the university and do the studies. But I worked in the meantime. I worked in Baia-Sprie for about two years until 1950. And then in 1950, they moved us to [INAUDIBLE], which is Baia-Mare. And then I went to the center of the company, which I worked there. I had a hired position already there. And I was already a chief of department there in organization and planning. So I worked there. And then I moved. I wasn't married yet. So I rented a room there in-- a place there in-- because there was-- under the communists, it was very hard. You know, they didn't let you have more than one room or so. If you had two rooms, then one of you had to rent it out. So I rented out one room from another family. And I was there for a-- in that town in Baia-Mare, for about six years. In this time, my brother got married in '48. And he lived in Baia-Sprie, still there in my parents' house. That was where I was born. So he lived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And always for-- in the beginning, I went to them for Shabbat, and the weekends, because you're working there. Everybody was working Shabbat too, you know? You had to work six days a week, even seven, because Sundays we had meetings in whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was going there, and visiting to them. And then, they moved us to Satu-Mare, which became the region of that-- of that part-- that northern part of Transylvania. And I was working there as chief of department in the-- for the government. And I was there about till '57. And then in '57, and they moved back. The region moved to Baia-Mare. They were changing all the time, the regions. The capital city of the region was Baia-Mare. So they moved us there. And they gave us some help to-- for the key people in the company to build houses.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I built a house for myself. They gave-- they gave me a loan. And I wasn't married yet. And then later on in '61, I got married. Where did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife in Oradea. And I met her there in the winter of '61. And then in-- and in the spring, we got engaged. And-- and in the summer, in August, we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- you have one daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my daughter was born in '62. Next year, she was born in Baia-Mare.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much of your story did your-- does your daughter know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, I was just talking to you, interestingly, yesterday. And we were trying together here this afternoon. And-- I was-- and she was telling me, oh, great, my children will be able to see when they get a little bigger, the tape. And I said to her, you know, I was-- I'm sorry I didn't tell you when you were smaller. I was telling you later, because in the beginning I couldn't talk about the story. And she said, no, you are not right.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I was five, you were telling me already what's happened. I said, really? I was even thinking, at that young age I was talking to her about it. Did your experiences have influence on how you raised your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, a lot of people when they came back, they didn't believe in anything. They left their faith, especially if they were religious before, they were not religious after this. And we were, too, some, but I always felt I want to raise my daughter in the religious way. And, naturally, in Romania we couldn't do that like here. We were kosher at home. And-- but that was very hard to be kosher. My wife had to go to the-- to the [NON-ENGLISH] to-- with the chicken. And we got once a week, if we were able to get some kosher meat. You know, it was hard then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about synagogues and education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the synagogues-- we had the synagogue there. Saturday, we were working. We couldn't go to synagogue Saturday. But we went Friday evening. And some evenings, we were able to do it. And I know when I was in that position, you know, I had hard time at Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, because they didn't allow you to go. But I was always able-- Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur-- to go to the synagogue and not to work. I was-- I made myself sick, or whatever, I called in sick, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And education-- there were no Jewish schools there after the war. And my daughter, I always felt I had to come out from there, because what's going to be with my daughter? And we will lose her, you know? So after the war, some people left earlier. But there were periods-- some periods they let you out. And then they stopped. And, plus, there were a lot of problems, because they were throwing out the people from-- whoever wanted to leave, they were throwing them out from their jobs.

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't find other jobs, because everything was government jobs. And some people, when they put in application to leave, right away they throw them out from their jobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tell you, after my brother left in '66-- in '65, actually, I was-- I said, I must leave, you know? I'm not going to stay anymore. And I had nobody there from family. I had only one brother. But my wife had still her family in Oradea-- her mother, sister, and her sister who was born after the war. And her mother, you know, escaped in Budapest. But her father was in labor camp. He escaped too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was always think-- at that time, I said to myself, I have to leave. And then my brother was writing home from here. He also send me letters. He encourage me to come out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went in December 31st, in '65-- on December 30th, actually, I put in application to the police for leaving. And that time, that period people were leaving pretty good, because they had arrangements with Israel. They paid them, actually, for-- we found out later, they paid for each family some money to let them out. And I went. I put in application. And I was lucky. For six months-- the passport came in May, '66. And for six months, they didn't notify my company, the police. I don't know how they didn't notify. Usually, they do. But they notified them in May. And the director, general director for my company, called me. And I was in good relations with him. I helped him always to make his speeches. He was also a member of the parliament. When he went to Bucharest, I helped him write the speeches, and whatever. And I made reports for him, when he went to the meetings, whatever. Plus, I had the position there as chief of department. And he let me stay on. And he called me. And he said, how-- I just-- I just found out something very disturbing. You put in application to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: How come you don't-- you don't have faith in our system? So I said to him, look, I had a family problem. Look, my brother left. And he's in America. And I told him, my parents-- my wife's parents are leaving, even they didn't leave at that time. And I said, I must leave. I can-- what I'm going to do? I'm going to divorce my wife and my daughter? I have to leave. So he said, OK, I understand you. And he says, but I ask you something. I'm going to give another engineer. And you have to teach him to take over your position.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for three months, I told the police chief, he can't give you the visa. You can't leave. So-- and [INAUDIBLE] three weeks, I'm going to send cars to you to pick you up. You come to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm going to send you to Bucharest to meetings. I said, to meetings? I have the passport. No, you have to go. And that's what he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was for another three months working for the company. In the meantime, I had to do a lot of things to resolve things, because they didn't allow you to take out anything. I had a house, which I built based with a loan from the bank.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to sell it to pay back the bank, because you couldn't leave otherwise. So I sold it for half the price, for somebody from my company, a Romanian guy. And I got some money. But they were watching you, what you do with the money. So I had to put it in the checking account there in-- and I left some money for my mother-in-law, and my father-in-law. And then we spent some. We bought some linens, some shirts, some suits. We thought-- we thought, we're coming here with nothing, we have something. And-- but they didn't allow you to bring any money out. So when-- and in another three months, we-- I got the visa, finally. And we were getting ready to leave Romania. And in December, they took us to Bucharest. We went to Bucharest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to these-- we got the visas. And then they didn't allow you only 70 kilograms for each person, 110 pounds, some clothing only, and nothing else. And they-- they-- just the wedding ring.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you come to here in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Romania, I left-- we went to Italy, and to Naples from Bucharest. We flew there. And in Naples, they sent us to-- [INAUDIBLE] took us over in Genoa. And there we were four months. We had-- we learned some English there. We had some English courses. And, you know, we letters to my brother, my cousins here, a few cousins there. And we were there for four months. And I remember it was very cold in Genoa, winter. And we didn't have enough heat. And my daughter got sick there. But we managed. And we came out in May. May 10th we arrived to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you received in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this country? I tell you, very well received. My brother was at the airport with my sister-in-law, and my wife's two aunts, which were here from before the war, and two cousins of mine-- one cousin who was working for the Jewish committee, [? Gelar. ?] He was waiting at the airport. Actually, he married my cousin in Germany after the war. He was in the military there. And they were waiting for us. And they took us to-- we got a hotel. And we didn't know at that time what area is that, at Broadway, downtown, in a small hotel. We were there in one room with my daughter and my wife. Were there for about two months. And we were getting some help from the Joint-- from the HIAS, actually-- not HIAS, actually, NYANA. We were getting some help for food. And my wife cooked whatever she could there. She bought conserves. And she-- on an electric something, she did some. And I went right away to the NYU, to New York University. And I enrolled to-- I enrolled myself in an English intensive course. So I was there for about two months. I went the whole day. We were in the-- because that was even close. It was walking distance from Broadway, from that hotel to the university. And I was-- I was enrolled at NYU. And I graduated that course there. And then we-- my cousin, who was that director Jewish committee's wife, she found out in Astoria, in my wife's [INAUDIBLE], they find us an apartment, a small apartment, a rent-stabilized apartment, which we paid very little, $75 at that time a month, which was a lot of money for us at that time. And I was trying-- they were-- they were ready to send me-- because I had a degree in industrial engineering, they were trying to send me to Milwaukee, to a forest company. But, finally, I didn't want to go away from here, New York. So I-- finally, I found a place where my cousin's husband just opened up a small place. He was alone on 110th Street in Manhattan, a supply, [INAUDIBLE] supply place. And I got the job there. And first job, I had $100 a week. And that was pretty good. And then I got 150, I got 200. So I got-- and we were working together. And we established this business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you still?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm still in this business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever returned to any of the places that you were in in Germany, in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. And I said to my wife, I want to go back-- number one, I want to go back to Dachau, and say a Kaddish there for my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we went to-- I but I didn't want to sleep over in Germany. I couldn't sleep over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to Zurich. And from Zurich, we flew into Munich. And from there, we took the train. And we went to Dachau-- which is actually, the concentration camp is in a little town, Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Munich, Allach, Dachau, and then Dachau. So we went with the train. It's about half an hour ride from Munich to Dachau. And we went to the camp. And there, they rebuild-- they have a barrack. Actually, I have a picture from that camp. They have a barracks there. And that is a flame, so-- and there is a-- there is a Jewish flame there, a memorial there. So I said Kaddish there. And I show my wife there in Dachau. And we saw the crematorium there-- she saw, because I knew it from before. And so I wanted to be there. I went there for a few hours. And we went back to Munich. And we flew right to Budapest from there, and from Budapest to Romania. That time, my wife's family-- not her father, her father died. But her mother, and sister, and nephew, they were in Oradea. They were living still in Oradea.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to visit them. And my mother had to go to the cemetery there. And from there, we went to Baia-Sprie. A I always went to Baia-Sprie, whenever I went to Romania, to the cemetery, because I had my grandparents there buried, and my great-grandparents. So I felt I say Kaddish there, and I say it because I can't say for my parents, because there is no place for them. Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I survived probably to be able to tell the story. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And to be able to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] have a family, and [INAUDIBLE] the names for my parents, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] have their names. Thank God, I have the grandchildren. And my father has a name, Yehoshua, which is one of my grandchild. My grandparent has a name, which is Eliezer, the grandchild. And my mom has a name. My mother has a name. And my sisters, Blima, which is my daughter. And one of a grandchild has my little younger sister's name, Chana, Chana Vider. So I think-- I think God help me, because I never-- I never imagined I was going to survive. And it was really very few people who survived. And I think God wanted me to survive to be able to tell the story, and to have a family, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you dream about that period at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I dream? Yes, I was dreaming for a-- for a long years. I was-- I was dreaming I'm in the camp. And I'm-- I was dreaming for months and months, having dreams about it, not anymore. I'm not dreaming any more about the concentration camp, about-- sometimes, I'm dreaming with my father. I see him, and sometimes about my mother, not my sisters. I don't know, not my sisters. But sometimes my father and my mother comes back to me in dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to tell your story now, not earlier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I tell you. I was thinking to tell the story-- first, when I-- when I find out, you know, about the deniers, and I saw on television something. And I was ready to grab this guy and kill him, you know, when he was telling nothing happened. It's a hoax, and this kind of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to myself, where are my parents? Where are my sisters, my relatives? At least 30 or 40 people from my family So I decided I have to talk, number one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, number two, I have to talk to people, the children, the new generation. They must know what's happened. They must know what mankind could do. And they must know what's happened, and to be able to-- to-- if ever could happen something like this, to able to fight it, to fight against it, because I was thinking, if we knew something like this happened, if they would have announce on the radio, look, they're killing Jews, and they knew. They knew-- the newspaper, the government in America knew. In Britain, they knew. They knew they are killing Jews, and they are killing-- mass killings. And they could have bombed the concentration camps. They could have bomb the railroads. They could have-- they could have slowed down the killings. Nothing was done. And if we knew what's happening, we wouldn't have gone. They would have kill us. They would have kill a lot of us. But maybe some more would have escape. So it's very-- I decided it's very important to tell the new generation, to tell the children. And that's why I was talking about this in-- in several places. I had-- I made a speech-- we have every year, a memorial in the New Montefiore Cemetery in Long Island. For the [INAUDIBLE] Baia-Mare area, [INAUDIBLE] Oradea-Mare, Jews, for the Holocaust survivors, and for the memorial for the Holocaust-- who were killed in the Holocaust. We have a big memorial there. And every year, we-- some people, some survivors, one survivor makes a speech, and some children of survivors. So I spoke there about three years ago, about the Holocaust, some of-- you know, they give you only 15 minutes. So they have to do it concentrated. And-- and they ask me-- we-- in the summer-- we have a bungalow in the summer and near Fishkill near Poughkeepsie. And there were over 50 families there. They asked me Tisha B'Av to speak about the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I spoke one Tisha B'Av night. And the young people, especially, were very interested to hear this. And then, when I heard about the Spielberg, I was a little late calling up. I called up, I think a year and a half ago, something like that. I wanted-- I said to myself, I want to give a testimony. And I wanted to be able to-- the world to know what's happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I forgot something tell you. You were asking about the holidays in the camps. And I'm just remembering-- I told you about Yom Kippur. Once we were in Allach, in the concentration camp. And I was working in the kitchen already. And we knew it's Yom Kippur evening, starts Yom Kippur. And we were praying in the kitchen there, in the place where we were peeling the potatoes. And we had the [INAUDIBLE]. We were more than 10 there, actually. We were starting praying. And at that evening when we were praying, I tell you, from the other concentration camp near us, there were some Russian prisoners there. They threw some stones to us. And I said to myself, look at that. Even here, it could happen this. And I-- and I was fasting the whole Yom Kippur. I didn't eat nothing. And that holiday, I remember that's what we-- we kept.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we've kind of come to the end of your tape. We're going to show the pictures that you had. And that will sort of be the end of the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
